title: Proposed Solutions
text:
Solution A — Agentic Web Search




🤖


<div class="sol-label">Solution A · Zero-History Products</div>
<div class="sol-title">Agentic Web Search with Citation</div>
<div class="sol-reach">Targets <strong>47</strong> rows with no purchase history</div>




When a product has no purchase history at all, Step 5 has no anchors. The only signal available is the product description, manufacturer, and catalog number. An agentic pipeline can autonomously enrich these rows:



Query construction — build a structured search from RecognizedProductDesc, RecognizedManufacturerName, and RecognizedProductCatalogNumber.


Search & extract — agent calls a web search tool or medical-device catalogue API and extracts candidate UOM labels and typical pack sizes.


Citation attachment — every suggestion is stored with its source URL so analysts can verify before accepting.


Confidence flagging — agent rates its own confidence (“exact catalog number match” vs. “description keyword match”). Low-confidence results require mandatory analyst sign-off before write-back.


Feedback loop — accepted suggestions are written to purchase history so future runs resolve the same products without a search.



High effort Requires analyst review Self-improving over time Needs prompt fine-tuning

title: Proposed Solutions
text:
Solution B — Similar-Product Transfer




🔗


<div class="sol-label">Solution B · Sparse History Products</div>
<div class="sol-title">Purchase History Bridge via Product Similarity</div>
<div class="sol-reach">Targets <strong>31\,400</strong> rows with 1–4 unit history</div>




Rather than going to the web, look inside existing purchase history for structurally similar products and borrow their ThresholdV2 clusters as a prior:



Similarity candidates — find products in the catalogue sharing the same RecognizedManufacturerID, same RecognizedProductTypeID, and similar description tokens (keyword overlap or embedding distance).


UOM profile transfer — the similar product’s ThresholdV2 clusters and WAP are borrowed. The proximity scoring in Step 5 runs exactly as normal, using the borrowed thresholds.


Downgraded confidence — the resulting score is multiplied by a transfer discount factor (e.g., 0.80) to signal the assignment is inferred, not directly observed.


Analyst review queue — transferred rows surface in a separate queue for analysts to confirm the analogy is valid before the result propagates.



Medium effort Needs similarity criteria co-design with analysts No external API calls





Key questions for analysts
Before implementing, these must be answered collaboratively:

Does same manufacturer + product type constitute a strong enough analogy, or are there product categories where this would be systematically misleading?
What discount factor appropriately penalises borrowed confidence?
Are there clinical categories (implants vs. consumables) that require different similarity criteria?

title: Proposed Solutions
Proposed Solutions
text:
Recommended Roadmap



Medium Term


B — Similar-Product Transfer


Co-design similarity criteria with domain analysts. Pilot on one manufacturer/category before expanding. Borrow ThresholdV2 profiles from structurally similar products with rich history.




Longer Term


A — Agentic Web Search


Prototype on a small batch of zero-history products. Evaluate citation quality and build the analyst review workflow. Requires prompt engineering, de-duplication, and a write-back feedback loop before scaling.












Solution
Reach
Effort
Analyst Input Needed




B — Similar-product transfer
~31,400 rows
Medium
Yes — criteria definition


A — Agentic search
~47 rows
High
Yes — citation sign-off

title: UOM Recognition Improvements
text:
The Five-Step Recognition Pipeline
Each row passes through steps in order and exits as soon as one step succeeds. The rows in this dataset are those that survived all four earlier steps unmatched, making Step 5 the last line of defense.


Recognition Steps — in order of execution




1


Exact String


File UOM matched directly to known codes




2


Catalogue


Known product UOM lookup




3


Threshold V1


Legacy min/max price band rules




4


File UOM


Direct file-label UOM match




5


Inference ✦


DBSCAN clusters + proximity scoring

title: UOM Recognition Improvements
text:
Confidence Score Distribution
Every row resolved by Step 5 carries a proximity-derived confidence score. A score of 1.0 means the price fell squarely inside a DBSCAN cluster; 0.30 is the minimum accepted threshold.


                            
                                            





Score Band
Share of Recognized




≥ 0.90 — High confidence
76.6 %


0.70 – 0.90 — Moderate
12.6 %


0.30 – 0.70 — Marginal
10.8 %




77% of all Step 5 recognitions score above 0.90. The median is 0.99 and the mean is 0.92 — the inference engine is not just broad, it’s highly confident on the rows it does resolve.


Continue to Deep Dive to understand exactly how the proximity formula works and why certain rows remain unresolved — or jump to Proposed Solutions for actionable next steps.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works
text:
Legacy Steps 1–4 use hand-crafted or naively computed min/max price bands. A single extreme transaction widens a V1 band to the point of uselessness — or, when data is sparse, the range is too tight to match new, slightly-different prices.



DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) scans the full purchase history for a product and groups prices into tight, organically-shaped clusters while ignoring statistical outliers as noise. Each resulting ThresholdV2 record captures:







Field
What it stores




MinThreshold / MaxThreshold
Observed price band of the dense cluster — no hand-tuning


QuantityOfEAInUOM
The UOM quantity this cluster represents (e.g. 12 for BX/12)


MeanEA
Per-each price anchor, used when no WAP entry exists



Because outliers are excluded, the clusters are precise. A product with a BX/12 at $120–$180 and an EA at $10–$15 produces two separate clean clusters — not one wide band.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works
text:
Legacy Steps 1–4 use hand-crafted or naively computed min/max price bands. A single extreme transaction widens a V1 band to the point of uselessness — or, when data is sparse, the range is too tight to match new, slightly-different prices.



DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) scans the full purchase history for a product and groups prices into tight, organically-shaped clusters while ignoring statistical outliers as noise. Each resulting ThresholdV2 record captures:







Field
What it stores




MinThreshold / MaxThreshold
Observed price band of the dense cluster — no hand-tuning


QuantityOfEAInUOM
The UOM quantity this cluster represents (e.g. 12 for BX/12)


MeanEA
Per-each price anchor, used when no WAP entry exists



Because outliers are excluded, the clusters are precise. A product with a BX/12 at $120–$180 and an EA at $10–$15 produces two separate clean clusters — not one wide band.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works
text:
Part 2 — The Proximity Inference Formula
Once DBSCAN clusters exist, Step 5 must decide which cluster best explains the file’s PriceUOM. The core insight: price operates on a log scale. A $5 difference matters far more at a $10 product than a $10,000 product.

Step A — Log-distance from cluster edge

logPrice = log₁₀(max(PriceUOM, 0.0001)) logLo = log₁₀(max(MinThreshold, 0.0001)) logHi = log₁₀(max(MaxThreshold, 0.0001))  logDist = 0 ← price is inside the cluster band = logPrice − logHi ← price is above the band = logLo − logPrice ← price is below the band

If PriceUOM falls inside the cluster, logDist = 0 — a perfect match. Outside, the distance grows as the log ratio of the price to the nearest edge.


Step B — Gaussian proximity score

proximityScore = exp(−0.5 × (logDist / LOG_EPS)²)  where LOG_EPS = 0.25 (≈ 1.78× tolerance from the cluster edge)

This is a Gaussian kernel on log-price space. At logDist = 0 the score is exactly 1.0. At a 1.78× price deviation from the edge the score falls to ~0.37. At 2× it collapses to ~0.02 — effectively zero.


Why This Mirrors Analyst Reasoning


Analysts manually verify a UOM by asking: “is this price in the right ballpark for this product and pack size?” A BX/10 should cost ~10× a single unit. If the file shows 3× or 20× off, the analyst rejects it. The proximity score formalises this intuition — prices inside the cluster score 1.0, prices slightly outside drop gracefully, and wildly mismatched prices collapse to near zero. Working in log space means the tolerance is proportional (a 50% deviation at $10 and a 50% deviation at $10,000 are treated identically), which is exactly how analysts think about pricing.




Step C — Four candidate pools
Step 5 scores up to four candidate sources and picks the highest:









#
Source
Signal
Min score required




1
DBSCAN cluster
Density-derived from purchase history
None (primary)


2
Known Product UOM (WAP)
Weighted-average price from history
None


3
EA/1 WAP
Per-each price derived from WAP ÷ qty
None


4
File UOM × WAP
File-stated qty × per-each WAP
0.85 (stricter — file labels unreliable)



The row is assigned if the winning score is ≥ 0.30; otherwise it exits unrecognized.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works
text:
Winner-Source Breakdown


                            
                                            


DBSCAN clusters win 94% of the time — validating that density-based thresholds are the most reliable signal. The remaining 6% are split across three supplementary pools, each catching a class of products DBSCAN alone doesn’t yet cover.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works
text:
Top 10 Assigned UOMs


                            
                                            


EA/1 (single-unit) is the most commonly assigned UOM — confirming that many suppliers quote at per-each pricing even when the historical record has only larger-pack purchases. BX and CA in quantities of 10, 12, 50, and 100 round out the top assignments, reflecting the standard medical-supply pack sizes in this dataset.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works
text:
Why Unrecognized Rows Stay Unrecognized
Three root causes account for nearly all 53,754 unresolved rows:



🚫


3,168


No Usable PriceUOM


Null or zero — Step 5 cannot compute log-distance without a price




📉


31,447


No / Sparse History


Zero or 1–4 units — not enough data to form reliable DBSCAN clusters




🎯


21,900


Price Out of Range


History exists but file price falls below 0.30 proximity score for every cluster




Purchase History Depth Comparison


                            
                                            


The gap between recognized and unrecognized is sharpest in the “Some (5–49)” and “Strong (≥ 50)” bands — these rows have adequate history for DBSCAN but their file prices deviate beyond the 0.30 proximity threshold. This pattern points to price drift, new pack sizes, or supplier unit-vs-case mismatches as the primary culprit for the remaining unrecognized population.

See Proposed Solutions for concrete strategies to address each root cause.
